target.name: Sunfire
target.founder: Carl Berninghausen, Nils Aldag, Christian von Olshausen

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series E → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.4x) to $315M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Climate & Energy (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 19 same-sector peers exist in Climate & Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

data.narrative:
Sunfire is a Series E climate and energy company that has raised $315M+ to date. The valuation analysis draws from a comp universe of 9 private frontier-tech peers—including TAE Technologies, Redwood Materials, Helion, Commonwealth Fusion Systems, and QuantumScape—plus one relevant public comparable (LNZA).

Stage-progression methodology is the primary applicable lens here. Using total capital raised ($315M) as a baseline proxy and applying a 1.4x step-up multiplier typical of Series E to subsequent-stage progression yields $441M. This is the sole quantitative anchor, given that a sufficiently dense peer-median approach cannot be constructed; while 19 Climate & Energy peers exist sector-wide, none satisfy narrow same-stage or adjacent-stage filters with sufficient sample size (minimum three required), and a broad all-sector median would introduce unwarranted noise.

The triangulated valuation range is $375M (low) to $507M (high), with a median of $441M. This single-method basis introduces material uncertainty. Key risks to valuation include technology maturation risk, capital intensity of scaling, and the early-stage nature of the Climate & Energy sector's public comparable, which constrains direct market-based validation. The absence of narrow peer multiples means the range relies heavily on stage-typical step-ups rather than peer trading multiples. Investors should treat $441M as a reasonable midpoint pending further data on unit economics, pathway to revenue scale, and sector-specific exit multiples.
